Article.ArticleTitle: Zone 3 Aggressive Retinopathy of Prematurity in a Near-Term Delivered Big Baby With a Birth Weight of 3,200 g.

Abstract.AbstractText: Aggressive retinopathy of prematurity (A-ROP) represents a severe, rapidly progressive variant of retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) that is most observed in extremely premature, low-birth-weight infants and usually involves the posterior zones. Its occurrence in near-term infants with normal birth weight, involving Zone 3, is rare. This report dwells upon such a rare occurrence. This is a case of a male baby born at 37 weeks' gestation with a birth weight of 3,200 g. The baby required a neonatal ICU (NICU) admission for 13 days for respiratory distress and seizures. At a postmenstrual age of 40 weeks, fundus examination revealed A-ROP in both eyes in Zone 3. After parental counseling, bilateral intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) therapy with bevacizumab (one-third adult dose, 0.4 mg/0.015 mL) was administered. The infant showed rapid regression of plus disease and hemorrhages. Subsequent follow-ups demonstrated continued improvement, with complete retinal maturation by six months. At the final follow-up, the child had a mature retina, an unremarkable anterior segment, and a refraction of +0.50 DS/-2.00 DC × 180° in both eyes. This case highlights a rare presentation of A-ROP involving Zone 3 in a near-term, normal birth-weight infant. The potential role of postnatal factors, including respiratory distress management, in the development of A-ROP outside traditional risk profiles. The prognosis is good if the condition is treated promptly and there is no further systemic deterioration.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Human subjects: Informed consent for treatment and open access publication was obtained or waived by all participants in this study. Conflicts of interest: In compliance with the ICMJE uniform disclosure form, all authors declare the following: Payment/services info: All authors have declared that no financial support was received from any organization for the submitted work. Financial relationships: All authors have declared that they have no financial relationships at present or within the previous three years with any organizations that might have an interest in the submitted work. Reference.Citation: Retinopathy of prematurity: a global perspective of the epidemics, population of babies at risk and implications for control. Gilbert C. Early Hum Dev. 2008;84:77–82.
International classification of retinopathy of prematurity, third edition. Chiang MF, Quinn GE, Fielder AR, et al. Ophthalmology. 2021;128:0–68.
The International Classification of Retinopathy of Prematurity revisited. Classification of Retinopathy of Prematurity. Arch Ophthalmol. 2005;123:991–999.
Retinopathy of prematurity: two distinct mechanisms that underlie zone 1 and zone 2 disease. Flynn JT, Chan-Ling T. Am J Ophthalmol. 2006;142:46–59.
Aggressive posterior retinopathy of prematurity: a review on current understanding. Kumawat D, Sachan A, Shah P, Chawla R, Chandra P. Eye (Lond) 2021;35:1140–1158.
Survey on retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) in Italy. Borroni C, Carlevaro C, Morzenti S, et al. Ital J Pediatr. 2013;39:43.
Aggressive posterior retinopathy of prematurity in infants ≥ 1500 g birth weight. Sanghi G, Dogra MR, Katoch D, Gupta A. Indian J Ophthalmol. 2014;62:254–257.
Aggressive posterior retinopathy of prematurity in large preterm babies in South India. Shah PK, Narendran V, Kalpana N. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed. 2012;97:0–5.
India to gear up to the challenge of "third epidemic" of retinopathy of prematurity in the world. Sai Kiranmayee P, Kalluri V. Indian J Ophthalmol. 2019;67:726–731.

Retinopathy of prematurity in Asian Indian babies weighing greater than 1250 grams at birth: ten year data from a tertiary care center in a developing country. Vinekar A, Dogra MR, Sangtam T, Narang A, Gupta A. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17699940/ Indian J Ophthalmol. 2007;55:331–336.
